attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset of severe lower abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. She is eight weeks pregnant based on her last menstrual period. She has a history of one previous caesarean section. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears pale and diaphoretic. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is tender in the right lower quadrant with guarding. Vaginal examination reveals cervical motion tenderness and scant blood in the vaginal vault.

Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin: 95 g/L (115-160)
Serum β-hCG: 2500 IU/L
Transvaginal ultrasound: No intrauterine gestational sac, right adnexal mass with free fluid in the pelvis
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate surgical management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms highly suggestive of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy, including severe abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, tachycardia, and hypotension. The transvaginal ultrasound findings of no intrauterine gestational sac, a right adnexal mass, and free fluid in the pelvis further support this diagnosis. Given her unstable condition, immediate surgical intervention is necessary.

Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the most appropriate surgical management in this case. It involves the complete removal of the affected fallopian tube and is the preferred approach for ruptured ectopic pregnancies or in women not desiring future fertility. Salpingectomy provides definitive treatment and reduces the risk of persistent trophoblastic tissue and recurrent ectopic pregnancy in the same tube.

Laparoscopic salpingostomy is a more conservative surgical approach where the ectopic pregnancy is removed through an incision in the fallopian tube, leaving the tube intact. This is a more technically difficult surgical procedure, has an increased risk of haemorrhage and also likely preserves a damaged Fallopian tube which has greater risk for recurrent ectopic pregnancy. Salpingostomy is usually reserved for cases where a woman has already lost a Fallopian tube, where the other Fallopian tube appears damaged or there is a history of infertility. It is not the best option in this case of a likely ruptured ectopic with haemodynamic instability.

Methotrexate injection is a medical management option for early, unruptured ectopic pregnancies in haemodynamically stable patients. It is contraindicated in this case due to the patient's unstable condition and the likelihood of a ruptured ectopic pregnancy.

Dilation and curettage is a procedure used for management of incomplete miscarriages or termination of intrauterine pregnancies. It is not indicated for ectopic pregnancies and could be dangerous in this scenario.

Laparoscopic oophorectomy involves the removal of an ovary. Most ectopic pregnancies are located in the Fallopian tube not the ovary. Rarely ovarian ectopics can occur - however this is unusual.

In summary, the patient's clinical presentation, laboratory results, and ultrasound findings are consistent with a ruptured ectopic pregnancy requiring immediate surgical intervention. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the most appropriate management to control bleeding and definitively treat the ectopic pregnancy.
learningPoints: Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the preferred surgical approach for ruptured ectopic pregnancy or in women not desiring future fertility.
Transvaginal ultrasound is the gold standard for diagnosing ectopic pregnancy, with key findings including no intrauterine gestational sac and an adnexal mass.
references: Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Diagnosis and management of ectopic pregnancy (Green-top Guideline No. 21). London: RCOG; 2016. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/diagnosis-and-management-of-ectopic-pregnancy-green-top-guideline-no-21/
